claims.claim: When the post-acceptance decode tail is implemented as a variable-length sequence of small CUDA kernels, per-kernel launch overhead can dominate latency.

evidence_refs.quote: # Acceptance-Length CUDA Graph Bank: Reducing Launch Overhead in Speculative-Decoding Tails

claims.claim: On an NVIDIA GB10 (CUDA 13.0) microbenchmark simulating acceptance-length-dependent decode tails with up to 8 acceptance lengths and 4 phases per accepted token, the graph bank reduces mean per-request latency by 1.42×–2.34× over dynamic kernel launches across vector sizes n ∈ {256, 1024, 8192, 65536} and four request distributions.

claims.claim: CUDA graphs reduce kernel launch overhead by capturing a sequence of kernel launches into a single executable graph that can be replayed with near-zero host-side dispatch cost.

evidence_refs.quote: # Acceptance-Length CUDA Graph Bank: Reducing Launch Overhead in Speculative-Decoding Tails

claims.notes: Historical backfill linked the claim to already published artifacts; this is not independent source revalidation.

evidence_refs.quote: Speculative decoding accelerates autoregressive inference by proposing multiple candidate tokens per step, but the number of accepted tokens varies per request. When the post-acceptance decode tail is implemented as a variable-length sequence of small CUDA kernels, per-kernel launch overhead can dominate latency. This paper evaluates a *CUDA graph bank*—a set of pre-captured CUDA graphs, one per possible acceptance length—that selects and replays the graph matching the realized acceptance length

claims.notes: Historical backfill linked the claim to already published artifacts; this is not independent source revalidation.

claims.claim: graph_bank**: Pre-captures one CUDA graph for each acceptance length `1..maxL`.

evidence_refs.quote: # Acceptance-Length CUDA Graph Bank: Reducing Launch Overhead in Speculative-Decoding Tails

claims.notes: Historical backfill linked the claim to already published artifacts; this is not independent source revalidation.

claims.claim: Included as a negative control to quantify capture overhead.

evidence_refs.quote: A naive implementation launches a variable number of kernels per request, paying launch overhead proportional to the acceptance length. A single max-length guarded graph avoids variable launch counts but executes all kernels up to the maximum acceptance length, wasting work when the actual acceptance length is shorter. Per-request graph recapture incurs capture overhead that typically exceeds the launch savings.

claims.notes: Historical backfill linked the claim to already published artifacts; this is not independent source revalidation.

claims.claim: Default sweep parameters: - `maxL = 8` (maximum acceptance length) - `phases = 4` (kernels per accepted token) - Vector sizes: `n ∈ {256, 1024, 8192, 65536}` - Acceptance-length distributions: - **fixed1**: Always accept 1 token (worst case for launch overhead ratio).
